candidates.concept: Machine Learning as Function Finding (Three-Step Framework: Model, Loss, Optimization)
candidates.why_it_matters: The unifying mental model of the entire lecture: ML ≈ a machine automatically finding a function f. The three steps — (1) define a candidate function set (Model/H), (2) define a loss to score function quality (Loss), (3) optimize to pick f* = argmin L(f) — structure every later topic (CNN, Transformer, RL, gradient descent).
candidates.transcript_summary: Pages 2, 6, 8, 11, 14, 17, 25: ML defined as a machine automatically finding a function. Three steps repeated as a recurring scaffold: 設定範圍 (set scope = Model), 設定標準 (set standard = Loss), 達成目標 (achieve goal = Optimization). The summary slide maps each step to concrete techniques (Deep Learning, Supervised/RL, Gradient Descent).

candidates.concept: Gradient Descent Optimization (finding f* = argmin L(f))
candidates.why_it_matters: Step 3 of the framework: how the best function is actually found. Gradient descent (and variants Adam/AdamW) is the workhorse optimizer of deep learning. It is highly visualizable — iterating down a loss curve toward a minimum makes the abstract argmin concrete and exposes hyperparameters like learning rate.
candidates.transcript_summary: Pages 15, 17, 18: f* = argmin_{f∈H} L(f). Optimization algorithms listed as Gradient Descent (Adam, AdamW), Genetic Algorithm. Page 18 stresses hyperparameters (learning rate, batch size, initialization) and goals: lower L(f*), faster, insensitive to hyperparameters.

candidates.concept: Loss Functions and Supervised vs Semi-supervised Learning
candidates.why_it_matters: Step 2 of the framework: the loss L(f) quantifies how far a function's outputs are from the correct answers, defining what 'good' means. It depends on training data and labels, which distinguishes supervised, semi-supervised, and reinforcement learning. Without a loss there is nothing to optimize.
candidates.transcript_summary: Pages 11-13: L(f1) = sum of distances between f1's outputs and correct labels (worked example total = 15). Loss depends on training data. Supervised Learning uses labels; Semi-supervised Learning adds constraints from unlabeled data (e.g. similar-looking Pokémon should have similar battle power).

candidates.concept: Regression vs Classification vs Structured/Generative Learning
candidates.why_it_matters: Classifying ML tasks by the type of function output is the first practical decision when framing a problem: regression outputs a number, classification outputs a category, structured/generative learning outputs a structured object (image, sentence). It frames where modern systems like ChatGPT fit.
candidates.transcript_summary: Pages 3-5: Regression = numeric output (e.g. predict tomorrow's PM2.5); Classification = category output (e.g. spam Yes/No). Structured/Generative Learning produces structured objects. ChatGPT framed as generative learning decomposed into many classification problems.

candidates.concept: Overfitting: Training vs Testing and Regularization
candidates.why_it_matters: A low training loss does not guarantee good test performance — the model may 'memorize answers' (overfit). Understanding the train/test gap and remedies like regularization is essential for building models that generalize, and is one of the most demonstrable pitfalls in practice.
candidates.transcript_summary: Pages 19-20: A small L(f) on training data does not imply good test performance. Page 19 adds extra considerations to the loss (e.g. Regularization). Page 20 contrasts 'L small, tests well' vs 'L small, tests badly (memorizing answers)' — i.e. overfitting.

candidates.concept: Model Architectures as Candidate Function Sets (Fully-connected, CNN, Self-attention/Transformer)
candidates.why_it_matters: Network architectures (CNN, RNN, Transformer) are concrete instantiations of the 'candidate function set' H from Step 1; the choice is driven by task characteristics. This connects the abstract framework to the named architectures students encounter throughout deep learning.
candidates.transcript_summary: Pages 9, 20-24: A neural network's structure is the candidate function set H. Architecture is chosen by task characteristics. CNN vs Fully-connected and CNN vs Self-attention are shown. Cross-cutting techniques (Residual Connection, Batch Normalization, Cross-entropy loss) can support multiple steps.

selected_concepts: Machine Learning as Function Finding (Three-Step Framework: Model, Loss, Optimization)
Gradient Descent Optimization (finding f* = argmin L(f))
Overfitting: Training vs Testing and Regularization
selection_rationale: Selected for the strongest combination of pedagogical centrality and demo feasibility. (1) The three-step framework is the spine of the entire lecture (importance 10) and can be demonstrated end-to-end with a simple linear-regression example that touches Model, Loss, and Optimization in one notebook. (2) Gradient Descent (demo feasibility 10) is the most visualizable concept — animating iterates descending a loss curve makes argmin L(f) concrete and naturally introduces the learning-rate hyperparameter. (3) Overfitting (demo feasibility 9) is a high-impact, interactive cautionary concept: fitting polynomials of increasing degree and comparing train vs test error vividly shows that low training loss can fail to generalize, motivating regularization. Together these three form a coherent narrative arc (framework → how we optimize → why optimization alone is not enough) ideal for a single classroom-ready interactive notebook. Regression/Classification and architecture topics are excellent context but are either more conceptual (taxonomy) or less self-contained for an executable demo, so they are retained as candidates rather than selected.
assumptions: Source PDF is the slide deck for Hung-yi Lee's 2023 'Machine Learning — Basic Concepts' introductory lecture; slides are primarily in Traditional Chinese with English technical terms, and no separate instructor transcript was provided.
Demo feasibility scores favor concepts that can be illustrated with synthetic data and lightweight Python (NumPy/Matplotlib) without large datasets or GPUs, suiting a Colab classroom setting.
Pokémon battle-power (CP) regression and image examples in the slides are illustrative analogies; demos may substitute equivalent synthetic numeric examples for executability.
